questions.question_text: Which features does Databricks automatically enable for Delta Lake optimization without manual intervention?
options: Auto Optimize and Auto Compaction
Manual OPTIMIZE commands only
Z-Ordering on all columns
Daily VACUUM operations
questions.explanation: Auto Optimize and Auto Compaction run automatically to maintain optimal file sizes (around 128 MB) and prevent the small file problem that often plagues data lakes. These features work transparently during write operations without requiring manual intervention.

questions.question_text: What is the primary benefit of the Lakehouse Architecture?
options: Lower storage costs only
Combining data warehouse performance with data lake flexibility
Eliminating the need for SQL
Supporting only structured data
questions.explanation: The Lakehouse Architecture combines the best features of data lakes (low-cost storage and support for diverse data types) and data warehouses (performance and ACID guarantees) into a single platform, providing data warehouse-like performance and reliability on data lake storage.

questions.question_text: Which compute type should you use for interactive development and ad-hoc analysis?
options: Job clusters
SQL Warehouses
All-purpose clusters
Serverless compute only
questions.explanation: All-purpose clusters are designed for interactive development and ad-hoc analysis. They stay running between commands, making them ideal for notebook development where data scientists and engineers run exploratory queries iteratively.

questions.question_text: What is Photon acceleration particularly effective for?
options: Storage optimization only
Heavy computation and aggregations
Network transfers
Cluster startup time
questions.explanation: Photon acceleration provides vectorized query execution that can speed up queries by up to 12x. It's particularly effective for queries involving heavy computation, aggregations, joins, and data transformations.

questions.question_text: For production ETL pipelines with predictable workloads which cluster type is most cost-effective?
options: All-purpose clusters
Job clusters
High-concurrency clusters
Development clusters
questions.explanation: Job clusters are ephemeral clusters created specifically for running automated workloads. They start when a job begins, run the workload, and terminate automatically upon completion, making them highly cost-effective for scheduled ETL pipelines.

questions.question_text: Which instance types should you choose for shuffle-heavy operations with large joins?
options: Memory-optimized instances
Compute-optimized instances
Storage-optimized instances with local SSDs
GPU-accelerated instances
questions.explanation: Storage-optimized clusters with local NVMe SSDs (like AWS i3 or Azure L-series) excel at shuffle-heavy operations like large joins and aggregations, as they can use local SSDs for shuffle data, significantly improving I/O performance.

questions.question_text: Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) automatically performs which optimization?
options: Writes Delta files
Coalesces shuffle partitions based on runtime statistics
Creates indexes on tables
Manages cluster scaling
questions.explanation: AQE dynamically adjusts query plans during execution based on runtime statistics, including coalescing shuffle partitions to reduce the number of tasks, switching join strategies based on actual data sizes, and handling data skew more effectively.

questions.question_text: What is the key advantage of Serverless compute in Databricks?
options: Lower cost for always-on workloads
Zero cluster startup time with automatic optimization
Better performance than dedicated clusters
Unlimited concurrent users
questions.explanation: Serverless compute eliminates infrastructure management overhead while providing instant availability (zero startup time) and automatic optimization. Resources automatically scale based on workload demands and you only pay for actual compute time used.

questions.question_text: Unity Catalog provides which type of governance?
options: File-level only
Database-level only
Unified governance for all data and AI assets
Workspace-specific governance only
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog provides a single governance solution for all data and AI assets, offering fine-grained access controls at catalog, schema, table, and even row and column levels, with comprehensive data lineage across the platform.

questions.question_text: Which optimization does Z-Ordering provide?
options: Reduces file sizes
Co-locates related information in the same files
Increases write speed
Removes duplicate records
questions.explanation: Z-Ordering co-locates related information in the same set of files, dramatically improving query performance when filtering on specific columns by minimizing the amount of data that needs to be read.

questions.question_text: Data skipping in Delta Lake works by using what?
options: Secondary indexes
File statistics in the transaction log
Manual partition pruning
Bloom filters only
questions.explanation: Delta Lake automatically collects statistics about data files, including minimum and maximum values for each column within each file. These statistics are maintained in the Delta Lake transaction log and used to skip entire files that don't contain relevant data.

questions.question_text: SQL Warehouses are optimized specifically for which workloads?
options: Machine learning training
ETL processing
SQL workloads and BI tools
Stream processing
questions.explanation: SQL Warehouses (formerly SQL Endpoints) are optimized specifically for SQL workloads and business intelligence tools, providing instant query results through aggressive caching and query optimization, with automatic scaling based on query load.

questions.question_text: What is the primary benefit of using Databricks Connect?
options: Faster cluster startup
Local development with remote execution
Automatic code deployment
Built-in version control
questions.explanation: Databricks Connect enables you to write and execute Spark code on Databricks clusters directly from your local development environment, allowing you to use local IDEs with debugging capabilities while utilizing Databricks' computational power.

questions.question_text: Which magic command allows you to execute another notebook in Databricks?
options: %python
%run
%sql
%fs
questions.explanation: The %run magic command lets you execute other notebooks, enabling modular development where common functions are stored in separate notebooks and imported as needed.

questions.question_text: Auto Loader uses which mechanism for near real-time file detection?
options: Polling intervals only
Cloud provider notifications (AWS SQS Azure Event Grid)
Manual triggers
Database triggers
questions.explanation: For streaming ingestion, Auto Loader uses cloud provider notifications (like AWS SQS, Azure Event Grid) to detect new files within seconds of arrival, enabling near real-time processing.

questions.question_text: What does the cloudFiles.schemaLocation option store in Auto Loader?
options: Data files
Inferred schema and checkpoint information
Temporary cache
Query results
questions.explanation: The cloudFiles.schemaLocation option stores the inferred schema and checkpoint information, enabling schema evolution and exactly-once processing guarantees. This is critical for production use.

questions.question_text: Which option in Auto Loader captures malformed records without losing data?
options: ignoreCorruptFiles
rescuedDataColumn
maxFileAge
schemaHints
questions.explanation: The rescuedDataColumn option specifies a column where malformed records that don't match the expected schema are stored as JSON strings, preventing data loss while maintaining pipeline stability.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the display() function in Databricks notebooks?
options: Print to console
Enhanced visualization for DataFrames
Debug code
Export data
questions.explanation: The display() function provides enhanced visualization for DataFrames, including profiling statistics, data distributions, and automatic visualizations with customizable chart types, colors, and aggregations.

questions.question_text: Which feature allows multiple users to edit the same notebook simultaneously?
options: Version control
Real-time collaboration
Git integration
Notebook workflows
questions.explanation: Real-time collaboration in Databricks notebooks allows multiple users to edit the same notebook simultaneously, with presence indicators showing who's working where.

questions.question_text: What does the maxFilesPerTrigger option control in Auto Loader?
options: Total files to process
Files processed in each micro-batch
Maximum file size
File retention period
questions.explanation: The maxFilesPerTrigger option limits the number of files processed in each micro-batch, helping manage resource utilization and processing latency.

questions.question_text: Databricks notebooks support which programming languages natively?
options: SQL
Python
Java
Scala
R
questions.explanation: Databricks notebooks support SQL, Python, Scala, and R within the same notebook using language magic commands. Java and C++ are not natively supported in notebooks.

questions.question_text: Auto Loader supports which file formats?
options: JSON
CSV
Parquet
Video files
Avro
PDF
questions.explanation: Auto Loader supports JSON, CSV, Parquet, Avro, ORC, Delta, and other data formats. While it can handle binary files, it doesn't process video content directly. PDFs would be handled as binary files.

questions.question_text: The %debug magic command in notebooks is used for what?
options: Performance profiling
Interactive debugging after errors
Cluster monitoring
Code formatting
questions.explanation: The %debug magic command can be activated after an error occurs, opening an interactive debugging session where you can examine the stack trace and variable values at the point of failure.

questions.question_text: Which debugging tool provides task-level metrics in Databricks?
options: Notebook debugger
Spark UI
Ganglia
Variable explorer
questions.explanation: The Spark UI provides detailed information about job stages, tasks, and executors, including task-level metrics like execution time, shuffle read/write volumes, and spill to disk.

questions.question_text: What is the Bronze layer in the Medallion Architecture primarily used for?
options: Business aggregates
Raw data ingestion in original format
Cleaned and validated data
Real-time analytics
questions.explanation: The Bronze layer serves as the landing zone for all incoming data, preserving it in its original format with no transformations beyond format conversion, ensuring you can always reprocess from the original data if needed.

questions.question_text: Which layer in the Medallion Architecture implements data quality checks and deduplication?
options: Bronze
Silver
Gold
All layers equally
questions.explanation: The Silver layer contains validated, deduplicated, and standardized data. It performs data quality checks, removes or quarantines failed records, and implements deduplication logic.

questions.question_text: What is the primary advantage of Delta Live Tables (DLT) for ETL development?
options: Lower compute costs
Declarative pipeline development
Faster query execution
Manual optimization control
questions.explanation: DLT revolutionizes ETL development by providing a declarative framework where you define what the final datasets should look like, and DLT handles execution details, infrastructure, orchestration, and data quality automatically.

questions.question_text: DLT expectations are used for what purpose?
options: Performance optimization
Data quality rules and validation
Cost management
Schema evolution
questions.explanation: Expectations are declarative data quality rules that can warn, drop, or fail records based on conditions you define. DLT automatically tracks quality metrics showing how many records pass or fail each expectation.

questions.question_text: In a MERGE statement which clause handles record updates?
options: WHEN NOT MATCHED
WHEN MATCHED
WHEN DELETED
INSERT INTO
questions.explanation: WHEN MATCHED clause in MERGE operations handles updates to existing records based on matching conditions. WHEN NOT MATCHED handles inserts and WHEN MATCHED AND condition THEN DELETE handles deletions.

questions.question_text: Which PySpark window function would you use to access the previous row's value?
options: lead()
lag()
row_number()
rank()
questions.explanation: The lag() function in PySpark window operations accesses values from previous rows, while lead() accesses values from following rows. This is essential for time-series analysis and comparing current values to previous periods.

questions.question_text: What does the Gold layer in Medallion Architecture typically contain?
options: Raw data
Cleaned data
Business-level aggregates and KPIs
Temporary processing data
questions.explanation: The Gold layer provides curated, business-ready datasets optimized for specific use cases, including denormalized views, calculated KPIs, metrics, and measures that power dashboards and reports.

questions.question_text: Which operation in SQL creates a new table from query results?
options: CREATE TABLE
INSERT INTO
CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS)
ALTER TABLE
questions.explanation: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) creates new tables from query results, combining table creation and data population in a single statement.

questions.question_text: In DLT pipelines what decorator is used to define a table in Python?
options: @table
@dlt.table
@delta.table
@create_table
questions.explanation: The @dlt.table decorator in Python is used to define datasets in Delta Live Tables pipelines. It can include parameters for comments, partitioning, and table properties.

questions.question_text: Which DataFrame operation would you use for OLAP-style multi-level aggregations?
options: groupBy() only
ROLLUP and CUBE
pivot()
window()
questions.explanation: ROLLUP and CUBE operations generate multiple aggregation levels in single queries, essential for OLAP-style analysis. They create subtotals and grand totals across different dimension combinations.

questions.question_text: True or False: Views created with CREATE VIEW store the actual data
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Views do not store actual data; they store the query definition. When queried, views execute the underlying query to retrieve current data from the source tables.

questions.question_text: What is the benefit of using approx_count_distinct() over count_distinct()?
options: More accurate results
Trades accuracy for speed
Uses less memory only
Works with NULL values
questions.explanation: approx_count_distinct() uses approximate algorithms that trade perfect accuracy for significantly better performance and lower memory usage, ideal when exact counts aren't required.

questions.question_text: What is the key difference between Databricks Asset Bundles and traditional deployment?
options: Faster execution
Bundle-based deployment treating assets as cohesive units
Lower costs
Better performance
questions.explanation: DAB treats related assets (notebooks, libraries, configurations, job definitions) as a cohesive unit that can be developed, tested, and deployed together, with externalized configuration in YAML files.

questions.question_text: Where is the bundle configuration defined in Databricks Asset Bundles?
options: In notebooks
databricks.yml file
config.json
requirements.txt
questions.explanation: The databricks.yml file serves as the manifest that defines the bundle's structure and deployment behavior, containing metadata, variables, environment configurations, and resource definitions.

questions.question_text: What type of run should you use when only certain tasks failed in a workflow?
options: Complete rerun
Repair run
Retry run
Manual run
questions.explanation: Repair runs enable efficient recovery without reprocessing successful tasks. Databricks automatically identifies failed and downstream tasks that need to be rerun while maintaining the same job run ID.

questions.question_text: Which directory contains the main code assets in a Databricks Asset Bundle?
options: /config
/src
/tests
/databricks
questions.explanation: The src/ directory contains the main code assets including Python modules, SQL files, and notebooks, with libraries and utility functions in separate modules for reusability.

questions.question_text: What is the primary benefit of serverless compute for production workloads?
options: Lower costs for 24/7 workloads
Zero infrastructure management with automatic optimization
Dedicated resources
Manual scaling control
questions.explanation: Serverless compute eliminates infrastructure management overhead, provides instant availability, automatic scaling based on workload demands, and automatic performance optimization without configuration effort.

questions.question_text: In a repair run what happens to the cluster configuration?
options: Uses the failed cluster
Inherits from original run but starts fresh clusters
Creates new configuration
Uses default settings
questions.explanation: Repair runs inherit the cluster configuration from the original run but start fresh clusters, eliminating any corrupted state while maintaining consistency.

questions.question_text: What should you analyze in Spark UI to identify data skew?
options: Executor memory only
Task duration distribution
Network I/O
Cluster events
questions.explanation: Task duration distribution in Spark UI reveals data skew when a few tasks take much longer than others, indicating imbalanced data distribution across partitions.

questions.question_text: Which deployment pattern is recommended for bundles in production?
options: Direct to production
Development → Staging → Production
Manual deployment only
Ad-hoc deployment
questions.explanation: Environment promotion should follow a clear path from development to production with automated validation at each stage, using different configurations for each environment defined in databricks.yml.

questions.question_text: What indicates successful caching in the Spark UI Stages view?
options: Repeated stages
Skipped stages
Failed stages
Pending stages
questions.explanation: Skipped stages in Spark UI indicate successful caching, while repeated stages suggest missing cache opportunities that could improve performance.

questions.question_text: Bundle deployment with DAB is performed using which method?
options: Manual copying
UI CLI or REST API
Git push only
Notebook execution
questions.explanation: Pipelines are deployed through the UI, CLI, or REST API with configuration that specifies target schemas, compute resources, and runtime parameters.

questions.question_text: True or False: Serverless SQL Warehouses require cluster configuration
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Serverless SQL Warehouses require no cluster configuration - Databricks fully manages the infrastructure including provisioning, scaling, and optimization transparently.

questions.question_text: What information does the SQL tab in Spark UI provide?
options: Storage metrics
Query plans and execution metrics
Network statistics
Cluster health
questions.explanation: The SQL tab displays query plans and execution metrics for DataFrame operations, showing parsed logical plans, optimized logical plans, and physical execution strategies.

questions.question_text: What is the main difference between managed and external tables?
options: Performance only
Managed tables control data lifecycle external tables reference data
Storage format
Query syntax
questions.explanation: Managed tables give Databricks control over both metadata and data files (dropping the table deletes data), while external tables only manage metadata (dropping the table preserves data files).

questions.question_text: Which permission allows users to see objects exist but not read data?
options: SELECT
USE
MODIFY
CREATE
questions.explanation: The USE permission enables browsing and discovery, allowing users to see what objects exist without being able to read the actual data. SELECT permission is required to read data.

questions.question_text: In Unity Catalog permissions flow in which direction?
options: Bottom-up only
From catalogs to schemas to tables
Random inheritance
No inheritance
questions.explanation: Permissions flow from catalogs to schemas to tables, with grants at higher levels automatically applying to child objects. This hierarchical model simplifies permission management.

questions.question_text: Who can create and manage catalogs in Unity Catalog?
options: Any user
Metastore admins
Table owners
Data analysts
questions.explanation: Metastore admins manage the Unity Catalog metastore and its global policies, including creating and managing catalogs, the top-level containers for data assets.

questions.question_text: Where are audit logs stored in Unity Catalog?
options: External files
System tables in Delta format
Log files
Cloud storage only
questions.explanation: Audit logs are stored in system tables within Unity Catalog (like system.access.audit) in Delta format, making them queryable with SQL and automatically maintained by Databricks.

questions.question_text: Delta Sharing allows data sharing without what?
options: Authentication
Data movement or copying
Permissions
Audit logging
questions.explanation: Delta Sharing enables secure data sharing across organizations without data copying or movement. Recipients query data that remains in the provider's environment.

questions.question_text: What type of access do Delta Sharing recipients have to shared data?
options: Read-write access
Read-only access
Full control
Delete permissions
questions.explanation: Delta Sharing provides read-only access to recipients. They cannot modify shared data, which is a technical limitation that ensures data integrity and provider control.

questions.question_text: Data lineage in Unity Catalog captures dependencies at which level?
options: Table-level only
Column-level and table-level
Database-level only
File-level only
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog tracks both table-level and column-level lineage, showing how individual fields flow and transform through pipelines, essential for impact analysis and compliance.

questions.question_text: Which statement correctly revokes permissions in Unity Catalog?
options: DELETE permission FROM user
REVOKE privilege ON object FROM principal
REMOVE ACCESS FROM table
DROP PERMISSION ON schema
questions.explanation: REVOKE statements remove permissions using the syntax: REVOKE privilege ON object FROM principal. The CASCADE option can revoke dependent grants.

questions.question_text: What is Lakehouse Federation primarily used for?
options: Data copying
Querying external databases without moving data
File storage
Cluster management
questions.explanation: Lakehouse Federation enables querying external databases without moving data, essential for hybrid architectures and gradual cloud migrations while maintaining data in source systems.

questions.question_text: True or False: Account admins have the highest level of control in Databricks
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Account admins hold the highest level of administrative control across the entire Databricks account, managing Unity Catalog metastores, identity federation, and account-level policies.

questions.question_text: Which costs are most significant when sharing data across different clouds?
options: Storage costs
Data transfer and egress costs
Compute costs only
Licensing fees
questions.explanation: Data transfer and egress costs represent the most significant expense in cross-cloud sharing, with providers charging substantial fees ($0.08-0.12 per GB) for data leaving their networks.

questions.question_text: What is the Delta Lake transaction log (_delta_log) used for?
options: Storing raw data files
Recording every transaction as ordered JSON entries to maintain ACID properties
Caching query results
Managing cluster state
questions.explanation: The Delta Lake transaction log (_delta_log directory) stores every transaction as ordered JSON entries (0000.json; 0001.json etc.) capturing adds; removes; and metadata changes. This ordered log is the foundation of Delta Lake's ACID guarantees and enables time travel.

questions.question_text: Which statement about Delta Lake ACID transactions is TRUE?
options: Delta Lake supports only eventual consistency
Concurrent reads and writes are coordinated using optimistic concurrency control
Only one writer is allowed at a time
ACID applies only to batch writes
questions.explanation: Delta Lake uses optimistic concurrency control to coordinate concurrent reads and writes. Writers attempt transactions optimistically and the log is checked for conflicts at commit time; ensuring serializable isolation without blocking readers.

questions.question_text: True or False: The VACUUM command in Delta Lake permanently removes files that are still referenced by the current table version
options: True
False
questions.explanation: VACUUM only removes data files that are no longer referenced by any version within the retention threshold (default 7 days). It never removes files referenced by the current version; making it safe to run regularly for storage reclamation.

questions.question_text: Which Delta Lake feature allows you to query a table as it existed at a prior point in time?
options: Schema evolution
Time travel using VERSION AS OF or TIMESTAMP AS OF
OPTIMIZE with ZORDER
ANALYZE TABLE
questions.explanation: Delta Lake time travel lets you query historical snapshots using VERSION AS OF <version_number> or TIMESTAMP AS OF '<timestamp>' syntax. The transaction log retains history for the configured retention period; enabling audit; rollback; and reproducibility.

questions.question_text: Which of the following are valid ways to perform time travel queries on a Delta Lake table?
options: SELECT * FROM table VERSION AS OF 5
SELECT * FROM table TIMESTAMP AS OF '2024-01-01'
SELECT * FROM table@v5
SELECT * FROM table WHERE _commit_version = 5
RESTORE TABLE table TO VERSION AS OF 5
questions.explanation: Delta Lake supports multiple time travel syntaxes: VERSION AS OF; TIMESTAMP AS OF; and the shorthand @v<version> notation in table references. RESTORE TABLE rolls the table back to a prior version. Filtering on _commit_version is not a valid time travel mechanism.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of COPY INTO in Databricks?
options: Copying files between cloud storage buckets
Incrementally loading files into a Delta table with idempotent exactly-once semantics
Replicating tables across workspaces
Creating table backups
questions.explanation: COPY INTO provides idempotent incremental file ingestion into Delta tables. It tracks which files have already been loaded so re-running the command is safe; making it ideal for batch file ingestion workflows where files arrive in cloud storage.

questions.question_text: Which Auto Loader ingestion mode is recommended for very high file volumes with millions of files?
options: Directory listing mode
File notification mode
Batch mode
Checkpoint mode
questions.explanation: File notification mode uses cloud provider event services (AWS SQS/SNS; Azure Event Grid) to receive per-file notifications instead of listing the directory. This is far more scalable and efficient for very high file volumes where directory listing would be too slow or costly.

questions.question_text: When using Auto Loader with schema evolution what happens when a new column is detected?
options: The stream fails immediately
The new column is added to the target schema and the stream restarts automatically
The new column is silently dropped
A manual schema update is required
questions.explanation: With cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode set to 'addNewColumns' (default); Auto Loader detects new columns; updates the inferred schema stored at cloudFiles.schemaLocation; and restarts the stream automatically to incorporate the schema change without manual intervention.

questions.question_text: Which of the following are key differences between COPY INTO and Auto Loader?
options: Auto Loader supports streaming; COPY INTO is batch only
Auto Loader scales better to millions of files
COPY INTO uses SQL syntax; Auto Loader uses DataFrame API
Both support exactly-once semantics
Auto Loader requires a SQL Warehouse
questions.explanation: Auto Loader (Structured Streaming) and COPY INTO both provide exactly-once semantics and incremental ingestion. Key differences: Auto Loader supports streaming micro-batches and scales better to very large file counts using file notifications; while COPY INTO is a SQL-based batch command. Auto Loader does not require a SQL Warehouse.

questions.question_text: What does the MERGE INTO statement in Delta Lake accomplish?
options: Creates a new table from a query
Upserts data by matching source and target rows then applying conditional insert/update/delete actions
Merges two schemas together
Combines two clusters
questions.explanation: MERGE INTO (upsert) matches source rows to target rows using an ON condition; then applies WHEN MATCHED (update/delete) and WHEN NOT MATCHED (insert) clauses. This enables efficient SCD Type 1 updates and CDC patterns without full table rewrites.

questions.question_text: In Structured Streaming what does the trigger(availableNow=True) option do?
options: Runs the stream continuously
Processes all available data in micro-batches then stops; combining streaming semantics with batch cost efficiency
Triggers only one micro-batch
Disables checkpointing
questions.explanation: trigger(availableNow=True) processes all data currently available in the source across one or more micro-batches then gracefully stops the stream. This provides exactly-once Structured Streaming semantics at batch job cost; ideal for scheduled pipelines.

questions.question_text: Which DLT expectation action drops records that fail data quality rules but does NOT fail the pipeline?
options: expect()
expect_or_fail()
expect_or_drop()
expect_all()
questions.explanation: expect_or_drop() silently removes records that violate the expectation and records the drop count in DLT metrics. The pipeline continues processing clean records. expect() logs violations as warnings; expect_or_fail() halts the pipeline on any violation.

questions.question_text: What is a Streaming Table in Delta Live Tables?
options: A view that streams data to external systems
A DLT dataset that processes each record exactly once using append-only semantics
A table that auto-deletes old records
A temporary staging table
questions.explanation: Streaming Tables in DLT consume each input record exactly once using append-only incremental processing. They are ideal for ingestion layers and stateful operations. Materialized Views re-compute results from all data on each refresh; while Streaming Tables only process new data.

questions.question_text: True or False: In PySpark a narrow transformation like map() causes a shuffle across the network
options: True
False
questions.explanation: Narrow transformations (map; filter; select) operate on data within a single partition and require no data movement across the network. Wide transformations (groupBy; join; distinct) cause shuffles. Understanding this distinction is critical for optimizing Spark job performance.

questions.question_text: What is the role of a checkpoint location in Structured Streaming?
options: Storing processed data
Persisting stream state and progress so the stream can resume exactly where it left off after a failure
Monitoring stream throughput
Compressing output files
questions.explanation: The checkpoint location stores the stream's metadata; offsets; and state to durable storage. On restart after failure; Spark reads the checkpoint to determine exactly which data was last committed and resumes from that point; guaranteeing exactly-once processing end-to-end.

questions.question_text: In Databricks Workflows which trigger type runs a job on a fixed schedule?
options: File arrival trigger
Manual trigger
Cron-based schedule trigger
Continuous trigger
questions.explanation: Cron-based schedule triggers run jobs at defined intervals using standard cron syntax (e.g. every hour; every Monday at 8am). File arrival triggers respond to new data; manual triggers require a user or API call; and continuous triggers restart jobs immediately on completion.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Databricks Workflows task dependencies are correct?
options: Tasks can depend on multiple upstream tasks
A task only runs if all its upstream dependencies succeed by default
You can set a task to run only on failure of an upstream task
All tasks in a workflow must run sequentially
Workflows support fan-out and fan-in dependency patterns
questions.explanation: Databricks Workflows supports rich DAG-based dependency patterns: tasks can have multiple dependencies; run on success or failure of upstream tasks; and fan-out/fan-in patterns are fully supported. Tasks do NOT have to run sequentially; parallel execution of independent tasks is a key feature.

questions.question_text: What does the DLT pipeline mode 'Triggered' mean compared to 'Continuous'?
options: Triggered pipelines run indefinitely; Continuous pipelines run once
Triggered pipelines process available data then stop; Continuous pipelines run 24/7 with low-latency streaming
Triggered pipelines are slower than Continuous
There is no difference
questions.explanation: In Triggered mode a DLT pipeline processes all available data and then shuts down; suitable for batch workloads with scheduled execution. In Continuous mode the pipeline runs indefinitely processing data with low latency as it arrives; suitable for near-real-time requirements. Triggered mode is generally more cost-effective.

questions.question_text: What is the three-level namespace used in Unity Catalog?
options: Database.Table.Column
Catalog.Schema.Table
Workspace.Database.Table
Metastore.Catalog.Schema
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog uses a three-level namespace: catalog.schema.table (e.g. main.sales.orders). Catalogs are the top-level containers; schemas (databases) group related tables; and tables/views are the leaf objects. This hierarchy enables fine-grained access control at each level.

questions.question_text: Which Unity Catalog features help enforce data privacy for sensitive columns?
options: Column masking policies
Row filters
Dynamic data views only
Column-level permissions (GRANT SELECT on specific columns)
Table-level encryption keys
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog provides column masking (which replaces sensitive values with masked versions for unauthorized users); row filters (which restrict which rows users can see based on their attributes); and column-level SELECT grants. These work together to enforce fine-grained data privacy without duplicating tables.

questions.question_text: A data engineering team runs nightly batch ETL jobs that process 500 GB of raw JSON files. The jobs typically complete in 2 hours. Recently; the team noticed cluster startup adds 8 minutes of overhead each night. Which cluster configuration best balances cost and performance for this workload?
options: Use an all-purpose cluster that stays running 24/7
Use a jobs cluster with instance pools to reduce startup time
Use a serverless SQL warehouse
Use a single-node cluster with a large instance type
questions.explanation: Because the workload is a scheduled batch ETL job; a jobs cluster is the right choice over an always-on all-purpose cluster (which wastes money during idle hours). Pairing the jobs cluster with an instance pool pre-warms VMs; cutting startup time from ~8 minutes to ~2 minutes. A SQL warehouse is optimized for BI queries not ETL; and a single-node cluster cannot handle 500 GB efficiently.

questions.question_text: A team maintains a Delta Lake table that receives thousands of small append operations daily from IoT sensors. Over the past month; query performance has degraded significantly. A data engineer investigates and finds the table's transaction log contains references to over 50;000 small Parquet files. Which action will most directly improve query performance?
options: Run OPTIMIZE on the table to compact small files
Increase the cluster size used for queries
Convert the table from Delta to Parquet format
Drop and recreate the table with a larger partitioning column
questions.explanation: The root cause is the small file problem: thousands of tiny Parquet files force the query engine to open many file handles and read many footers. Running OPTIMIZE compacts these small files into larger; optimally-sized files (typically ~1 GB). This dramatically reduces I/O overhead. Increasing cluster size treats the symptom not the cause; converting away from Delta loses ACID benefits; and repartitioning doesn't address existing small files.

questions.question_text: A data engineer is tuning a pipeline that performs heavy aggregations and joins on a 2 TB dataset stored in Delta Lake. The pipeline currently runs on a standard DBR cluster and takes 4 hours. The engineer wants to evaluate Photon acceleration. Which scenario would benefit most from enabling Photon?
options: A pipeline that mostly runs Python UDFs for custom string parsing
A pipeline dominated by SQL-based aggregations; joins; and scans on wide tables
A pipeline that streams micro-batches from Kafka with minimal transformations
A pipeline that trains an ML model using scikit-learn on sampled data
questions.explanation: Photon is a C++-based vectorized query engine optimized for SQL-based operations like scans; joins; aggregations; and filters on Delta Lake tables. It accelerates native Spark SQL and DataFrame operations but does NOT accelerate Python UDFs; arbitrary Python libraries like scikit-learn; or workloads with minimal transformation logic. The scenario with heavy SQL aggregations and joins on a large dataset is the ideal candidate.

questions.question_text: A workspace administrator notices that several teams have created all-purpose clusters with wildly different Spark configurations; causing instability and unexpected costs. The admin wants to standardize cluster configurations while still allowing teams to run their workloads. Which approach best achieves this goal?
options: Revoke all cluster creation permissions and create clusters on behalf of each team
Define cluster policies that constrain instance types; autoscaling ranges; and Spark configurations; then assign policies to teams
Ask teams to follow a wiki page with recommended settings
Migrate all workloads to serverless SQL warehouses
questions.explanation: Cluster policies are the governance mechanism in Databricks workspaces for controlling cluster configurations. Policies let admins define allowable instance types; min/max workers; auto-termination settings; Spark configs; and more. Teams can still create clusters but only within policy constraints. Revoking permissions entirely blocks productivity; a wiki has no enforcement; and SQL warehouses are not suitable for all ETL or ML workloads.

questions.question_text: A data engineer sets up Auto Loader to ingest CSV files from a cloud storage landing zone. After running successfully for two weeks; the pipeline suddenly fails with a schema mismatch error. Investigation reveals that the upstream team added two new columns to the CSV files without prior notice. Which Auto Loader configuration best handles this situation going forward?
options: Set cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode to addNewColumns so new columns are automatically incorporated
Disable schema inference entirely and hardcode the schema
Switch from Auto Loader to a manual COPY INTO command
Set cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode to none and ignore new columns
questions.explanation: Auto Loader supports schema evolution modes that control behavior when new columns appear. Setting schemaEvolutionMode to addNewColumns allows the pipeline to automatically merge new columns into the target table's schema; preventing failures when upstream schemas change. Hardcoding the schema would require manual updates for every change; COPY INTO doesn't offer the same built-in schema evolution; and ignoring columns risks data loss.

questions.question_text: A company receives daily data dumps from three different vendors: Vendor A sends gzip-compressed CSV files (~10 GB); Vendor B sends nested JSON with arrays (~5 GB); and Vendor C sends Apache Avro files (~20 GB). The data engineer needs to build ingestion pipelines into Delta Lake for all three. Which statement about file format selection for ingestion is correct?
options: All files should first be converted to Parquet before ingesting into Delta Lake
Auto Loader natively supports CSV; JSON; and Avro formats; so each vendor's files can be ingested directly
Only JSON files can be ingested by Auto Loader; CSV and Avro require custom parsers
Avro files cannot be stored in Delta Lake due to schema incompatibility
questions.explanation: Auto Loader (cloudFiles) natively supports multiple file formats including CSV; JSON; Avro; Parquet; and more. Each vendor's data can be ingested directly into Delta Lake without any intermediate conversion step. Delta Lake stores data as Parquet internally but handles the conversion automatically during write. There is no schema incompatibility between Avro and Delta.

questions.question_text: A data engineer needs to load a one-time historical backfill of 2 million Parquet files from cloud storage into a Delta Lake table. The files will not receive updates after the initial load. A colleague suggests using Auto Loader; but the engineer is considering COPY INTO instead. Which reasoning correctly supports choosing COPY INTO for this scenario?
options: COPY INTO is always faster than Auto Loader for any file volume
COPY INTO is simpler for one-time or infrequent batch loads where the overhead of setting up a checkpoint and streaming infrastructure is unnecessary
COPY INTO automatically handles schema evolution better than Auto Loader
Auto Loader cannot read Parquet files
questions.explanation: COPY INTO is well-suited for one-time or infrequent batch ingestion scenarios. It processes files idempotently (tracking which files have been loaded) without requiring a streaming checkpoint directory. For a one-time historical backfill with no ongoing incremental loads; the simplicity of COPY INTO is an advantage. Auto Loader excels at continuous or frequent incremental ingestion with its file notification and streaming checkpoint capabilities.

questions.question_text: A data engineer is ingesting semi-structured JSON data from an external API. Approximately 2% of the records contain malformed JSON (missing closing braces; unexpected characters). The engineer needs to ensure the pipeline does not fail on bad records but also needs to capture the malformed records for later review. Which approach best handles this requirement?
options: Set the mode option to PERMISSIVE and use the _corrupt_record column to capture bad rows
Set the mode option to FAILFAST to immediately stop on bad records
Use DROPMALFORMED mode and write a separate job to find bad records in the source
Wrap each record parse in a try-catch block using Python UDFs
questions.explanation: Spark's PERMISSIVE mode (the default for JSON) places successfully parsed fields into their respective columns and stores the entire malformed row in a special _corrupt_record column. This allows the pipeline to continue processing good data while preserving bad records for investigation. FAILFAST would crash the pipeline; DROPMALFORMED silently discards bad records making them hard to find; and Python UDF try-catch adds unnecessary complexity.

questions.question_text: A data engineer writes a MERGE INTO statement to upsert daily sales data into a target Delta table. During testing; they discover that some source records have duplicate keys; causing the MERGE to fail with an error about multiple source rows matching a single target row. Which approach resolves this issue?
options: Add a WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE clause
Deduplicate the source DataFrame before the MERGE by keeping only the latest record per key
Switch from MERGE to INSERT OVERWRITE
Increase the cluster size to handle the duplicates
questions.explanation: Delta Lake's MERGE requires that each target row matches at most one source row on the join condition. When the source contains duplicate keys; multiple source rows match the same target row; causing an ambiguity error. The fix is to deduplicate the source (e.g.; using a window function with ROW_NUMBER partitioned by the key and ordered by timestamp descending) before passing it to the MERGE. INSERT OVERWRITE replaces entire partitions which is a different pattern; and cluster size is unrelated.

questions.question_text: A data engineering team is designing a Delta Live Tables pipeline for their e-commerce platform following the medallion architecture. Raw clickstream events land in bronze; cleaned events go to silver; and business-level aggregates go to gold. The team wants the silver layer to reject records missing a required user_id field. Which DLT feature should they use?
options: Apply an expectation with @dlt.expect_or_drop('valid_user_id'; 'user_id IS NOT NULL') on the silver table definition
Add a CHECK constraint on the bronze table
Use a Python assert statement inside the transformation function
Create a separate validation pipeline outside of DLT
questions.explanation: DLT expectations are declarative data quality rules applied directly to table definitions. @dlt.expect_or_drop defines a quality constraint and automatically drops records that violate it; keeping the silver table clean. A CHECK constraint on bronze would reject writes to bronze rather than filter at silver; Python asserts would crash the pipeline on bad data; and a separate validation pipeline adds unnecessary complexity when DLT has built-in quality controls.

questions.question_text: A data engineer is building a Structured Streaming pipeline that consumes events from a Kafka topic. The Kafka producer occasionally sends duplicate events due to retry logic. The engineer needs to ensure the target Delta table contains no duplicates. Which approach is most appropriate for streaming deduplication?
options: Use dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark() with a watermark on the event timestamp to remove duplicates within a time window
Run a batch DISTINCT query on the target table after each micro-batch
Use the MERGE INTO statement directly inside a streaming query without foreachBatch
Rely on Kafka's exactly-once semantics to guarantee no duplicates reach the consumer
questions.explanation: dropDuplicatesWithinWatermark() is the native Structured Streaming operator for deduplication. It uses a watermark to define a bounded time window during which duplicates are tracked in state; making it memory-efficient. Running batch DISTINCT after each write is expensive and creates a race condition. MERGE cannot be used directly in a streaming query without foreachBatch. Kafka's exactly-once semantics do not cover producer-level retries that assign different offsets to the same logical event.

questions.question_text: A ride-sharing company processes trip completion events via Structured Streaming. Events sometimes arrive up to 2 hours late due to driver connectivity issues. The team needs to compute hourly trip counts that include late-arriving data while eventually cleaning up old state. Which configuration correctly handles this requirement?
options: Set a watermark of 2 hours on the event timestamp and use a window-based aggregation with append output mode
Do not set a watermark and use complete output mode to recompute all results every micro-batch
Set a watermark of 0 seconds to process events as they arrive
Use batch processing with a 2-hour scheduling interval instead of streaming
questions.explanation: A watermark of 2 hours tells Spark to wait up to 2 hours for late data before finalizing a window's result. Combined with a window aggregation and append output mode; finalized windows are emitted only after the watermark passes them; ensuring late events within the threshold are included. Complete mode without a watermark recomputes everything and never cleans up state; a 0-second watermark drops all late data; and batch processing introduces latency and doesn't handle late arrivals gracefully.

questions.question_text: A data engineer's nightly Databricks Workflow job fails intermittently at the third task in a five-task DAG. The error logs show 'SparkException: Job aborted due to stage failure' with executor lost messages. The first two tasks (which read small reference tables) always succeed. The third task joins a 500 GB fact table with the reference data. Which is the most likely root cause and fix?
options: The reference tables have schema changes; fix by pinning the schema
The executors are running out of memory during the large join; fix by increasing worker instance size or enabling adaptive query execution
The job token has expired mid-run; fix by extending the token TTL
The Delta table's transaction log is corrupted; fix by running FSCK REPAIR TABLE
questions.explanation: Executor lost messages during a large join strongly indicate memory pressure: executors are being killed by the cluster manager (YARN/Kubernetes) for exceeding memory limits. The fix involves either choosing larger instance types with more memory; enabling Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) to optimize the join at runtime; or adding broadcast hints if one side is small enough. Schema changes would produce different errors; token expiry would show authentication errors; and transaction log corruption is rare and shows different symptoms.

questions.question_text: A data engineering team uses Databricks Asset Bundles (DABs) to manage their pipeline deployments across dev; staging; and production environments. A junior engineer asks why DABs are preferred over manually creating jobs through the UI. Which set of benefits correctly describes the advantage of DABs?
options: DABs automatically optimize query performance across environments
DABs provide version-controlled; CI/CD-friendly infrastructure-as-code definitions for jobs; pipelines; and cluster configs; enabling reproducible deployments
DABs eliminate the need for Unity Catalog in multi-environment setups
DABs replace the need for Delta Live Tables pipelines
questions.explanation: Databricks Asset Bundles define workspace resources (jobs; DLT pipelines; cluster configs) as code in YAML/JSON files stored in version control. This enables code review for infrastructure changes; CI/CD integration for automated deployments; environment-specific overrides (dev/staging/prod); and reproducibility. DABs do not optimize queries; replace Unity Catalog governance; or replace DLT — they orchestrate and deploy these components consistently.

questions.question_text: A data platform team needs to orchestrate a complex daily workflow: first ingest raw data (Task A); then run two independent transformation jobs in parallel (Tasks B and C); and finally produce aggregated reports (Task D) only after both B and C succeed. Which Databricks Workflows configuration achieves this?
options: Create four separate workflows; each triggered by a cron schedule with staggered start times
Create one workflow with a linear chain: A → B → C → D
Create one workflow where Task D depends on both Task B and Task C; and both B and C depend on Task A
Run all four tasks as cells in a single notebook
questions.explanation: Databricks Workflows supports DAG-based task dependencies. By setting B and C to both depend on A; they run in parallel after A completes. Setting D to depend on both B and C creates a fan-out/fan-in pattern where D runs only after both parallel tasks succeed. Separate workflows with staggered schedules are fragile and error-prone; a linear chain forces B and C to run sequentially; and a single notebook loses independent failure handling and parallelism.

questions.question_text: A retail company has a DLT pipeline that processes point-of-sale transactions. During business hours (8 AM to 10 PM); transactions arrive continuously and dashboards must reflect data within 5 minutes. Overnight; only batch reconciliation jobs run. The team wants to minimize costs. Which DLT pipeline mode strategy is most appropriate?
options: Run the pipeline in Continuous mode 24/7
Run the pipeline in Triggered mode with a 5-minute cron schedule during business hours and a single nightly run for reconciliation
Run two separate pipelines: one in Continuous mode during business hours and one in Triggered mode overnight
Use Triggered mode once per day at midnight
questions.explanation: A Triggered mode pipeline with a frequent (5-minute) cron schedule during business hours provides near-real-time freshness while allowing the cluster to terminate between runs if processing finishes quickly. Overnight; a single triggered run handles reconciliation. Running Continuous 24/7 wastes resources during off-hours. Two separate pipelines add operational complexity. A single daily run violates the 5-minute freshness requirement during business hours.

questions.question_text: A data analyst reports they can query the table catalog_a.schema_sales.orders but receive an 'Access Denied' error when querying catalog_a.schema_hr.employees. The analyst's group has USE CATALOG on catalog_a and USE SCHEMA on schema_sales. Which Unity Catalog permission is most likely missing?
options: SELECT on catalog_a.schema_hr.employees
USE CATALOG on catalog_a
CREATE TABLE on schema_hr
MODIFY on catalog_a.schema_sales.orders
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog requires a chain of permissions to access an object: USE CATALOG on the catalog; USE SCHEMA on the schema; and the object-level privilege (e.g.; SELECT). The analyst already has USE CATALOG and can access schema_sales; so the catalog permission is fine. The missing piece is either USE SCHEMA on schema_hr or SELECT on the employees table (or both). Since the error is Access Denied on the specific table; granting SELECT on catalog_a.schema_hr.employees (along with USE SCHEMA on schema_hr) resolves the issue.

questions.question_text: A pharmaceutical company wants to share anonymized clinical trial results with three external research partners who do not have access to the company's Databricks workspace. The data must be shared securely without copying files to external storage. Which Databricks feature is designed for this use case?
options: Export the data as CSV files and email them to partners
Use Delta Sharing to create an open-protocol share that external recipients can access with any compatible client
Grant the partners direct access to the Unity Catalog metastore
Publish the data to a public S3 bucket with a presigned URL
questions.explanation: Delta Sharing is an open protocol for secure data sharing that allows external recipients to access shared data without needing a Databricks workspace. The data provider creates a share and recipient objects in Unity Catalog; and recipients use any Delta Sharing-compatible client (Spark; pandas; Power BI; etc.) to read the data. This avoids insecure methods like emailing CSVs or public buckets; and does not require giving external users direct metastore access.

questions.question_text: A data governance team discovers that a critical revenue metric in a gold-layer dashboard has been showing incorrect values since last Tuesday. They need to determine which upstream transformation or data source introduced the error. Which Unity Catalog capability is most helpful for this investigation?
options: Time travel to query the gold table as of last Monday
Column-level lineage in Unity Catalog to trace the revenue metric back through upstream tables and transformations
Row-level security policies on the gold table
Table access audit logs showing who queried the table
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog's column-level lineage tracks how data flows from source tables through transformations to downstream tables and dashboards. By examining the lineage of the revenue metric column; the governance team can trace it back through silver and bronze layers to identify exactly which transformation or source table changed. Time travel shows historical data but not the cause; row-level security is unrelated; and audit logs show access patterns not data flow.

questions.question_text: A healthcare company stores patient records in a Delta Lake table within Unity Catalog. Analysts need to query the table for aggregate reporting; but regulations prohibit them from seeing raw Social Security numbers and dates of birth. The company wants a single table (not separate views per team) with dynamic masking based on user group membership. Which approach meets these requirements?
options: Create column masking functions that return masked values for unauthorized groups and apply them to the SSN and DOB columns
Create a separate view for each analyst team that excludes sensitive columns
Use row-level security to hide rows containing SSN values
Apply dynamic column masking policies in Unity Catalog tied to group membership using SQL functions
questions.explanation: Unity Catalog column masking allows administrators to define SQL functions that dynamically transform column values based on the querying user's group membership. When an analyst in a restricted group queries the table; SSN might appear as '***-**-1234' and DOB as null; while privileged users see the real values. This is applied to the single table — no need for separate views per team. Row-level security filters rows; not columns; and separate views create maintenance overhead and data duplication of logic.

questions.question_text: How does Delta Lake use the transaction log to guarantee atomicity?
options: By locking files during writes
By using append-only ordered commits to the transaction log
By using a centralized metadata server
By requiring all operations to be performed sequentially
questions.explanation: Delta Lake guarantees atomicity through an append-only transaction log where each commit is represented by a small JSON or parquet file that captures what changed. The transaction log records operations such as file additions, deletions, schema changes, and metadata updates in an ordered sequence. When a writer finishes a transaction, it writes a new commit file to the log. If the commit file appears atomically, then readers either see the table before the commit or after it—never a partial state, thereby ensuring atomicity.

questions.question_text: What is the primary mechanism behind Delta Lake's Optimistic Concurrency Control?
options: It locks files during writes to prevent conflicts
It assumes transactions won't conflict and validates at commit time
It uses a centralized lock manager to coordinate writes
It requires writers to obtain exclusive access before writing
questions.explanation: Delta Lake uses Optimistic Concurrency Control, which optimistically assumes that multiple writers can operate in parallel without conflicts. Instead of locking, writers proceed with their changes and then, at commit time, Delta Lake reads the transaction log to check if another writer has modified the same parts of the table since the writer started. If a conflict is detected, the commit fails, and the writer must retry with a fresh snapshot.

questions.question_text: What types of operations might cause conflicts in Delta Lake's Optimistic Concurrency Control?
options: Two readers accessing the same data simultaneously
Two writers modifying completely different partitions
Two writers trying to modify the same data files concurrently
A reader accessing data while another reader uses a different filter
questions.explanation: In Delta Lake, conflicts typically occur when two writers try to modify or rewrite the same data files (e.g., the same partition of a table) concurrently. Other common conflict scenarios include concurrent schema changes or metadata-heavy operations. Readers don't conflict with other readers or writers due to snapshot isolation, and writers modifying different partitions generally don't conflict unless they're changing metadata that affects the entire table.

questions.question_text: What is the main difference between a shallow clone and a deep clone in Delta Lake?
options: Shallow clone copies only metadata while deep clone copies both metadata and data files
Shallow clone works only on small tables while deep clone works on large tables
Shallow clone maintains a link to source while deep clone is completely independent
Shallow clone is read-only while deep clone allows writes
questions.explanation: The main difference between shallow and deep clones in Delta Lake is that a shallow clone copies only the metadata of the source Delta table and creates references to the same underlying data files, without physically duplicating them. A deep clone, on the other hand, physically copies both the underlying data files and the transaction log to a new location, creating a fully independent copy of the table.

questions.question_text: Which Delta Lake indexing optimization technique helps with multi-column filtering?
options: Partitioning
Bloom filters
File optimization
Z-ordering
questions.explanation: Z-ordering is a multi-dimensional clustering technique that helps improve data locality for queries that filter on multiple columns. It places related data close together on storage, reducing I/O by clustering related data. After writing or updating a table, you can run OPTIMIZE ... ZORDER BY (col1, col2) to rearrange data files. This is especially beneficial for columns frequently used in filter predicates across multiple dimensions.

questions.question_text: What is the primary benefit of Bloom filters in Delta Lake?
options: They compress data to reduce storage costs
They allow for more efficient file pruning during queries
They guarantee unique values in a column
They improve write performance for large datasets
questions.explanation: The primary benefit of Bloom filters in Delta Lake is that they enable more efficient file pruning during queries. A Bloom filter is a probabilistic data structure attached to files that allows for fast checking of whether a file might contain certain values. If a bloom filter indicates that a file does not contain values of interest, that file can be skipped during query execution, reducing the amount of data that needs to be read.

questions.question_text: What are appropriate file sizes when optimizing Delta tables for performance?
options: Many small files (1-10MB) for maximum parallelism
Medium-sized files (~100MB) for balanced performance
Large files (~1GB) for efficient read operations
Very large files (>10GB) to minimize metadata overhead
questions.explanation: When optimizing Delta tables, consolidating many small files into fewer larger files of an optimal size (commonly ~1GB) improves read efficiency and reduces metadata overhead. Too many small files degrade query performance due to overhead in listing and opening files, while excessively large files can reduce parallelism. The OPTIMIZE command can be used with or without Z-Ordering to achieve well-sized, efficient data files, typically around 1GB in size.

questions.question_text: What is a key consideration when choosing columns for partitioning in Delta Lake?
options: Always choose high-cardinality columns for maximum pruning
Choose columns with moderate cardinality that appear in WHERE clauses
Avoid using date/time columns for partitioning
Always partition by multiple columns to improve performance
questions.explanation: When choosing partition columns in Delta Lake, it's important to select columns with moderate cardinality that frequently appear in WHERE clauses. Too few distinct values (low cardinality) result in skewed, large partitions, while too many (high cardinality) lead to many small files and overhead. The chosen column should distribute data relatively evenly. Finding a balance between pruning efficiency and manageability is key.

questions.question_text: What does the OPTIMIZE command do in Delta Lake?
options: Updates statistics for the query optimizer
Consolidates small files into larger ones
Removes duplicate records from the table
Creates new indexes automatically
questions.explanation: The OPTIMIZE command in Delta Lake consolidates many small files into fewer larger files of an optimal size (commonly ~1GB). This process, also known as compaction, improves read efficiency and reduces metadata overhead. Running OPTIMIZE with or without Z-Ordering can achieve well-sized, efficient data files. It does not update statistics (that's handled by the transaction log), remove duplicates, or create indexes.

questions.question_text: When would you choose NOT to partition data in Delta Lake?
options: When data volume is small (under 1GB)
When no single column is a good partition candidate
When using Z-Ordering on the table
When the table is only used for batch processing
questions.explanation: You might choose not to partition data in Delta Lake when no single column is a good partition candidate - for instance, when there's no column with appropriate cardinality or when queries are complex and filter on multiple columns with no clear pattern. In such cases, relying on Z-Ordering can still cluster data effectively. While partitioning provides coarse pruning by skipping entire directories, Z-Ordering can partially compensate for this by improving data locality.

questions.question_text: How do you enable Change Data Feed (CDF) in Delta Lake?
options: Set a table property when creating or altering the table
Use a special write mode when writing to the table
Enable it at the database level for all tables
It's automatically enabled for all Delta tables
questions.explanation: To enable Change Data Feed (CDF) in Delta Lake, you set a table property when creating or altering a Delta table. You can enable it by running: ALTER TABLE myDeltaTable SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true); or at creation time with: CREATE TABLE myDeltaTable (...) TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true);. Once enabled, you can read changes using the table_changes function or options like readChangeFeed.

questions.question_text: What happens if you try to write data that violates a schema constraint in Delta Lake?
options: The write succeeds but the violating records are marked as corrupt
The write fails with an error
The write succeeds but the violating records are automatically filtered out
The write succeeds and the schema is automatically updated to accommodate the new data
questions.explanation: If you try to write data that violates a schema constraint in Delta Lake (such as a NOT NULL constraint or a CHECK constraint), the write operation fails with an error. Delta Lake enforces schemas at write time. If a column doesn't match the expected data type or required columns are missing, the write fails. This strict enforcement helps maintain data quality and prevents accidental schema drift or data corruption.

questions.question_text: What is the primary difference between coalesce and repartition in Spark?
options: coalesce increases partitions while repartition decreases them
coalesce avoids a full shuffle while repartition always performs a full shuffle
coalesce preserves partitioning while repartition changes it
coalesce works only on RDDs while repartition works on DataFrames
questions.explanation: The primary difference between coalesce and repartition in Spark is that coalesce tries to minimize data movement by avoiding a full shuffle when reducing the number of partitions, while repartition always performs a full shuffle (unless it's a no-op). Coalesce merges existing partitions without redistributing data evenly, which can lead to skew if drastically reducing partitions. Repartition, on the other hand, ensures data is evenly distributed across the specified number of partitions but at the cost of a full shuffle.

questions.question_text: When would you use repartitionByRange instead of standard repartition?
options: When you need to reduce the number of partitions quickly
When you want to distribute data evenly by value ranges of specified columns
When working with streaming data
When you want to avoid a shuffle operation
questions.explanation: You would use repartitionByRange instead of standard repartition when you want to distribute data evenly by value ranges of specified columns. This is particularly useful when performing operations like sort-merge joins or range queries, as it ensures each partition contains a range of values based on the specified column(s). This pre-sorts or pre-clusters data according to a certain key or set of keys, potentially optimizing downstream operations that rely on sorted or range-based data distribution.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of the rebalance operation in Spark?
options: To reduce the number of partitions without shuffling
To evenly redistribute data across partitions without specifying keys
To sort data within each partition
To change the partitioning scheme of a DataFrame
questions.explanation: The purpose of the rebalance operation in Spark is to evenly redistribute data across the specified number of partitions without specifying particular keys. It's useful after a filter or other transformation that might result in an uneven distribution. Rebalance performs a full shuffle to achieve a uniform distribution and avoid skew, but unlike repartition or repartitionByRange, it doesn't provide control over partitioning by key or range.

questions.question_text: How can you control the size of output part-files when writing a Spark DataFrame to disk?
options: Use a file format that automatically compresses data
Set the target file size in the write options
Adjust the number of output partitions before writing
Use the coalesce method after writing
questions.explanation: To control the size of output part-files when writing a Spark DataFrame to disk, you should adjust the number of output partitions before writing. Since each partition typically becomes one output file (for file formats like Parquet), you can control the approximate file size by using df.repartition(n) or df.coalesce(n) to define how many output partitions Spark will have before calling df.write(...). By choosing n based on total data size and desired file size, you can achieve appropriately sized part-files.

questions.question_text: What is the most efficient approach for Type 1 updates in a Delta Lake table?
options: Delete and reinsert the entire record
Use the MERGE INTO command
Use INSERT OVERWRITE with a predicate
Use a combination of DELETE and INSERT
questions.explanation: The most efficient approach for Type 1 updates (overwriting existing records) in a Delta Lake table is to use the MERGE INTO command. This allows you to atomically update existing rows and optionally insert new ones if they don't exist, all in a single transaction. The MERGE operation is optimized in Delta Lake to maintain ACID guarantees while providing better performance than separate DELETE and INSERT operations or full table overwrites, especially for large tables with selective updates.

questions.question_text: How does watermarking help in stateful streaming operations?
options: It ensures all events arrive in perfect order
It bounds state size by allowing old state to be discarded
It guarantees exactly-once processing semantics
It prevents duplicate records in the output
questions.explanation: In stateful streaming operations, watermarking helps bound the size of the state by defining a threshold for how long to wait for late data. Watermarking tells Spark to drop state for keys that haven't received new data within a given threshold, preventing indefinite growth of state for old keys. Without watermarks, state would grow unbounded as new keys are continuously processed, eventually leading to memory issues or degraded performance in long-running streaming jobs.

questions.question_text: What is the purpose of broadcast in a stream-static join?
options: To reduce network traffic between nodes
To distribute the static dataset to all workers for local lookups
To ensure the streaming data is processed in order
To limit the amount of data processed in each micro-batch
questions.explanation: The purpose of broadcast in a stream-static join is to distribute a copy of the entire static dataset to all worker nodes. This is done when the static dataset is small enough to fit in memory. Broadcasting allows lookups to be performed locally on each executor without shuffling data, which significantly improves join performance. This pattern is particularly useful when enriching a streaming dataset with relatively small reference or dimension data.

questions.question_text: What approach can be used to deduplicate data in Structured Streaming?
options: Use distinct() on the streaming DataFrame
Use dropDuplicates() with watermarking
Use a stateless filter operation
Use MERGE operation at the sink
questions.explanation: To deduplicate data in Structured Streaming, you can use dropDuplicates() with watermarking. This is a stateful operation where Spark keeps track of keys seen so far, along with a watermark to remove old keys from state. For example: `stream_df.withWatermark("eventTime", "10 minutes").dropDuplicates(["key", "eventTime"])`. The watermark allows Spark to eventually clean up state for old events, preventing unbounded state growth while still effectively deduplicating the stream.

questions.question_text: How does Change Data Feed (CDF) help with propagating deletes?
options: It automatically removes deleted records from downstream tables
It explicitly records deletions with _change_type = 'delete'
It uses VACUUM to clean up deleted records
It provides a separate deletion log
questions.explanation: Change Data Feed (CDF) in Delta Lake helps with propagating deletes by explicitly recording deleted records in the feed with _change_type = 'delete'. This gives you explicit information about which rows were removed from the source table. Without CDF, identifying which records were deleted between versions would be complex. With CDF enabled, you can easily identify these deleted records and apply the same deletions to downstream consumers, maintaining consistency across your data pipeline.

questions.question_text: What problem does proper partitioning solve when archiving or deleting data?
options: It prevents data loss during deletion operations
It enables removal of data at the directory level without scanning all data
It automatically compresses old data
It maintains table statistics after deletions
questions.explanation: Proper partitioning solves the problem of efficiently archiving or deleting data by enabling operations at the directory level without scanning all data. For example, if data is partitioned by date, removing data older than a certain date can be done by simply dropping entire partition directories (e.g., `ALTER TABLE myDeltaTable DROP PARTITION (date < '2023-01-01')`). Without partitioning, you'd need to read the entire dataset, filter rows, and rewrite it—a more costly operation, especially for large datasets.

questions.question_text: How do 'smalls' (tiny files) impact Spark query performance?
options: They improve parallel processing capabilities
They decrease storage costs but increase compute time
They create file listing overhead and excessive tasks
They only impact performance when using cloud storage
questions.explanation: 'Smalls' (tiny files) negatively impact Spark query performance by creating file listing overhead and excessive tasks. Each Spark job starts by listing files in the input directories, and thousands of small files make this process slow. Spark creates one task per partition/file chunk, so many small files lead to many small tasks, increasing overhead and management complexity. This results in wasted CPU and memory resources as executors spend more time on overhead rather than productive data processing.

questions.question_text: What pattern is recommended for Stream-Static joins in Spark?
options: Load static data at the beginning of each micro-batch
Use broadcast to distribute static data to all workers
Store static data in external databases
Convert static data to a stream before joining
questions.explanation: For Stream-Static joins in Spark, the recommended pattern is to use broadcast to distribute static data to all workers. When the static dataset is small enough, broadcasting it to all executors allows lookups to become local operations without shuffling data. This significantly improves performance as it eliminates network transfer during the join operation. The pattern typically involves: loading the static data once, broadcasting it using functions like broadcast(), and then joining it with the streaming DataFrame.

questions.question_text: What is the Bronze-Silver-Gold pattern in Data Lakehouse architecture?
options: A naming convention for different storage tiers
A data modeling paradigm for dimension tables
A multi-hop architecture for data quality and refinement
A backup strategy for critical datasets
questions.explanation: The Bronze-Silver-Gold pattern in Data Lakehouse architecture is a multi-hop architecture for data quality and refinement. It organizes data processing into layers: Bronze (raw ingested data, append-only), Silver (cleansed, filtered, and conformed data), and Gold (aggregated or feature-engineered data for reporting, analytics, ML). This pattern allows for incremental quality improvements and transformations as data moves through the layers, with each layer serving different use cases and users.

questions.question_text: How do you handle schema evolution in Structured Streaming pipelines?
options: Restart the stream with each schema change
Use schema inference and mergeSchema options
Create a new stream for each schema version
Schema changes are not supported in Structured Streaming
questions.explanation: To handle schema evolution in Structured Streaming pipelines, you can use schema inference and mergeSchema options. When reading from a source like Delta Lake, you can set the option "mergeSchema" to "true" to allow the schema to evolve as new columns are added. For more complex changes, you might need to use a more flexible schema (like storing data in a semi-structured column) or implement logic to handle different schema versions. Proper schema management is crucial for long-running streaming applications.

questions.question_text: What is the best practice for checkpoint locations in Structured Streaming?
options: Use local file system for fastest performance
Use a new location for each stream restart
Use a stable location in cloud storage
Avoid checkpointing for better throughput
questions.explanation: The best practice for checkpoint locations in Structured Streaming is to use a stable location in cloud storage (like S3, ADLS, or GCS). Checkpoints contain critical information about the stream's progress, so they need to be persistent and accessible even if the cluster is terminated and restarted. Using cloud storage ensures durability and accessibility. It's also important to use a dedicated location for each streaming query and not to delete checkpoints if you want to resume processing from where it left off.
