A Direct Vector Access index does not have an automatic source table sync; instead the application must explicitly upsert or delete vectors via the Vector Search REST API or SDK. Delta Sync is preferred when the source data changes frequently and automation is desired.

questions.question_text: Which MLflow feature allows you to define a custom Python class with predict() logic to wrap a RAG chain for deployment?
options: mlflow.tracking.MlflowClient
mlflow.pyfunc.PythonModel
mlflow.models.ModelSignature
mlflow.data.DatasetSource
questions.explanation: mlflow.pyfunc.PythonModel is the MLflow abstraction that allows developers to define custom inference logic in Python by subclassing it and implementing the predict() method. This is the standard approach for wrapping complex RAG chains; custom pre/post-processing logic; or multi-model ensembles into a single deployable MLflow model.

questions.question_text: What is 'context precision' as an evaluation metric in a RAG pipeline?
options: The percentage of retrieved chunks that are relevant to the query
The proportion of ground-truth relevant information present in the retrieved context
The accuracy of the embedding model on a benchmark dataset
The ratio of correct tokens to total tokens generated
questions.explanation: Context Precision measures what fraction of the retrieved chunks are actually relevant to answering the query (precision = relevant retrieved / total retrieved). A low context precision means the retriever is returning noisy irrelevant chunks that may mislead the generator. It complements Context Recall; which measures how much relevant information was successfully retrieved.

questions.question_text: When deploying a custom LLM chain using Databricks Model Serving; what file must be included in the logged MLflow model to specify Python package dependencies?
options: requirements.yaml
conda.yaml or requirements.txt (specified via pip_requirements or conda_env)
dependencies.json
packages.lock
questions.explanation: MLflow model deployments require dependency specifications so the serving environment can recreate the correct Python environment. This is done by passing pip_requirements (a list or path to requirements.txt) or conda_env (a conda environment specification) when calling mlflow.*. log_model(). Without this; the serving endpoint may fail due to missing packages.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of 'differential privacy' techniques when preparing training data for fine-tuning foundation models?
options: To speed up the training process
To add mathematical noise guarantees that prevent the model from memorizing and leaking individual training examples
To compress the training dataset
To balance class distribution in the training set
questions.explanation: Differential privacy adds calibrated statistical noise during training to provide formal mathematical guarantees that the model cannot memorize or reproduce specific training examples. This protects the privacy of individuals whose data is included in the fine-tuning dataset and is particularly important in regulated industries (healthcare; finance) where training data may contain sensitive PII.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is correct?
options: RLHF trains a reward model from human preference data and uses it to fine-tune the LLM via reinforcement learning
RLHF only applies to computer vision models
RLHF eliminates the need for any pre-training of the base model
RLHF is identical to supervised fine-tuning on labeled data
questions.explanation: RLHF involves three stages: supervised fine-tuning on demonstration data; training a reward model from human preference comparisons; and then using reinforcement learning (typically PPO) to optimize the LLM's policy against the reward model. This process aligns model outputs with human preferences for helpfulness; harmlessness; and honesty. It is distinct from standard supervised fine-tuning which does not use a reward model.

questions.question_text: Which of the following are valid use cases for MLflow Experiment Tracking in a generative AI development workflow?
options: Logging prompt templates as parameters for comparison across runs
Recording evaluation metrics such as faithfulness and answer relevancy per run
Storing inference table data from production traffic
Comparing chunking strategies by logging retrieval quality metrics across experiments
Automatically retraining the LLM when metrics degrade
questions.explanation: MLflow Experiment Tracking is used to log and compare parameters (such as prompt templates; chunk size; overlap) and metrics (such as faithfulness; answer relevancy; context precision) across development runs. It supports artifact logging and run comparison. Production inference table data is managed by Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring rather than Experiment Tracking; and MLflow does not natively trigger automatic LLM retraining.

questions.question_text: What is the recommended approach for handling personally identifiable information (PII) discovered in documents ingested into a RAG pipeline?
options: Store PII documents without modification and rely on access controls alone
Apply PII detection and redaction or masking before embedding and indexing the documents
Delete all documents that contain any PII before ingestion
Only use PII-free public datasets for RAG applications
questions.explanation: The recommended approach is to apply automated PII detection (using tools such as Presidio or similar NLP-based detectors) followed by redaction or masking prior to embedding and indexing. This ensures PII does not get stored in the vector index or returned to end users in retrieved chunks. Relying solely on access controls is insufficient because the LLM itself could surface PII in generated responses.

questions.question_text: A data science team built a customer-support chatbot using a large foundation model with a simple prompt: 'Answer the customer question.' During testing; the chatbot frequently produces overly verbose answers; sometimes includes irrelevant product details; and occasionally fabricates warranty terms. Which prompt engineering fix is MOST likely to resolve these issues?
options: Increase the model's temperature to 1.0 to encourage more creative responses
Add explicit instructions with role assignment; output length constraints; and a directive to only reference provided context documents
Switch to a smaller model with fewer parameters
Remove the system prompt entirely and rely on the user message alone
questions.explanation: The root cause is an under-specified prompt. By assigning a clear role (e.g. 'You are a concise customer-support agent'); adding output length constraints (e.g. 'Respond in at most three sentences'); and instructing the model to only reference supplied context; you reduce verbosity; irrelevant content; and hallucinated warranty terms. Raising temperature would increase randomness and worsen the problem.

questions.question_text: A financial services company wants to build an AI agent that can answer questions about account balances; initiate wire transfers after manager approval; and cite compliance regulations. The agent must interact with three backend APIs in real time. Which design pattern is MOST appropriate?
options: A single zero-shot LLM call with all API documentation in the prompt
A ReAct-style agent with defined tools for each API; structured observations; and iterative reasoning steps
A static chain that always calls all three APIs in sequence regardless of the query
A fine-tuned model that has memorized all account balances
questions.explanation: A ReAct (Reasoning + Acting) agent is ideal because it interleaves reasoning steps with tool invocations; allowing the LLM to decide which APIs to call based on the user's query. This handles the dynamic nature of the task — some queries only need balance lookups while others require multi-step approval workflows. A static chain wastes resources calling unnecessary APIs; and memorizing balances is infeasible for live data.

questions.question_text: A startup must choose a foundation model for an internal code-review assistant. The assistant must understand code in 8 programming languages; handle files up to 15;000 tokens; and run within a limited GPU budget. Which selection criterion should weigh MOST heavily?
options: The model's performance on general-knowledge benchmarks like MMLU
The model's context window size and code-specific benchmark scores relative to its parameter count and serving cost
The number of languages the model can translate between in natural language
The model's image-understanding capabilities
questions.explanation: For a code-review assistant the critical factors are (a) sufficient context window to handle 15K-token files; (b) strong code understanding validated by code-specific benchmarks such as HumanEval or MBPP; and (c) cost-effective serving within a limited GPU budget — which favors smaller but code-specialized models. General-knowledge benchmarks and image capabilities are irrelevant to this use case.

questions.question_text: An engineer is building a question-answering system over technical manuals. For straightforward factual lookups ('What is the torque specification for bolt X?') the system works well; but for complex multi-step reasoning questions ('Compare the maintenance intervals of systems A; B; and C and recommend the most cost-effective schedule') it fails. Which approach should the engineer adopt?
options: Switch from chain-of-thought prompting to a single zero-shot prompt to simplify the pipeline
Implement chain-of-thought prompting that instructs the model to break the comparison into explicit reasoning steps before producing a final answer
Reduce the retrieval context window to return fewer chunks
Remove the system prompt to give the model more freedom
questions.explanation: Complex multi-step reasoning benefits from chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting; which instructs the model to decompose the problem into intermediate steps (retrieve intervals for each system; compare costs; then recommend). This explicit reasoning structure dramatically improves accuracy on comparison and synthesis tasks. Reducing context or removing guidance would hurt performance on these harder queries.

questions.question_text: A healthcare company deploys an LLM-based assistant that answers patient questions. During a red-team exercise; testers discover the model can be manipulated into providing specific dosage recommendations — which is clinically dangerous. Which guardrail implementation strategy BEST mitigates this risk?
options: Add a disclaimer at the end of every response stating 'consult your doctor'
Implement an input/output guardrail layer that detects medical-dosage requests via a classifier and blocks or redirects them before the response reaches the user
Fine-tune the model on more medical data so its dosage answers become accurate
Lower the temperature to 0.0 to make responses deterministic
questions.explanation: A dedicated guardrail layer with a topic classifier that intercepts both input and output is the most robust approach. It can detect dosage-related queries and either block them or replace the response with a safe referral message. Disclaimers alone are insufficient for safety-critical domains; fine-tuning does not guarantee correctness for all dosages; and temperature adjustment does not prevent harmful content.

questions.question_text: A legal team wants to build a RAG system over 500-page contracts. Each contract contains numbered clauses; sub-clauses; and cross-references between sections. The current chunking strategy uses fixed 512-token chunks with no overlap; and retrieval frequently returns partial clauses that miss critical cross-referenced context. Which chunking improvement is MOST effective?
options: Reduce chunk size to 128 tokens to increase the number of chunks
Switch to a structure-aware chunking strategy that splits on clause boundaries and includes parent-clause context in each chunk
Increase chunk size to 4096 tokens to capture entire sections
Remove chunking entirely and embed each full 500-page contract as one vector
questions.explanation: Structure-aware (hierarchical) chunking that respects clause boundaries ensures each chunk contains a complete legal thought. Including parent-clause context addresses cross-reference issues. Fixed-size chunking ignores document structure and splits mid-clause. Extremely large chunks dilute relevance; and embedding a full 500-page document exceeds embedding model limits and destroys retrieval precision.

questions.question_text: A team is building a RAG system for a multilingual knowledge base containing documents in English; French; German; and Japanese. They need to select an embedding model. Which selection criterion is MOST important?
options: Choose the embedding model with the largest output dimension regardless of language support
Select a multilingual embedding model (e.g.; multilingual-e5-large) that was trained on all four target languages and evaluate it on cross-lingual retrieval benchmarks
Always use a separate monolingual embedding model per language
Use a keyword-based TF-IDF model since it handles any language
questions.explanation: A multilingual embedding model trained on all target languages maps semantically similar content from different languages into a shared vector space; enabling cross-lingual retrieval. This is critical when users query in one language but relevant documents exist in another. Separate monolingual models cannot perform cross-lingual matching; and TF-IDF misses semantic similarity.

questions.question_text: An engineering team ingests PDF technical manuals that contain text paragraphs; specification tables; and circuit diagrams. Their current pipeline extracts only raw text using a basic PDF parser; resulting in garbled table data and completely lost diagram information. Which pipeline redesign MOST comprehensively addresses these issues?
options: Switch to a different text-only PDF parser with better encoding support
Implement a multi-modal document processing pipeline that uses table extraction (e.g.; table transformers); OCR for scanned pages; and a vision model to generate text descriptions of diagrams
Convert all PDFs to plain text and discard tables and diagrams
Only index the text portions and add a disclaimer that tables are not supported
questions.explanation: A multi-modal pipeline is needed because the documents contain three distinct content types. Table extraction tools preserve structured data; OCR handles scanned content; and vision models (or image captioning) convert diagrams into searchable text descriptions. Discarding tables and diagrams loses critical technical information that users will query.

questions.question_text: A media company wants to build a search system over a video library. Each video has a transcript; thumbnail images; and metadata tags. The team needs to make all content searchable via natural-language queries. Which approach correctly handles this multi-modal content for embedding and retrieval?
options: Embed only the transcripts and ignore images and metadata
Use a multi-modal embedding model (e.g.; CLIP-based) for images and a text embedding model for transcripts; then store both vector types in the same index with metadata filters
Convert thumbnail images to base64 text and embed them with a text model
Store only metadata tags and use keyword search
questions.explanation: The correct approach uses purpose-built models for each modality: text embeddings for transcripts and multi-modal embeddings (e.g.; CLIP) for images. Storing both in a unified vector index with metadata filters allows natural-language queries to match across modalities. Encoding images as base64 text produces meaningless text embeddings; and ignoring modalities reduces search coverage.

questions.question_text: A healthcare RAG pipeline ingests clinical notes that frequently contain patient names; dates of birth; and social security numbers. The team discovers that PII is appearing in retrieved chunks shown to end users. Which pipeline stage should be added; and where?
options: Add PII redaction as a post-processing step after the LLM generates its final answer
Add PII detection and redaction as a pre-processing step before chunking and embedding; so PII never enters the vector store
Add PII detection only at query time to filter out PII from user questions
Rely on the LLM's instruction-following to avoid repeating PII in answers
questions.explanation: PII must be detected and redacted before documents are chunked and embedded. This prevents PII from ever being stored in the vector index; eliminating the risk of retrieval surfacing it. Post-generation redaction is fragile because the LLM may rephrase PII in unexpected ways. Query-time filtering only protects against PII in questions; not in source documents. Relying on LLM instructions is unreliable for safety-critical requirements.

questions.question_text: A developer's RAG pipeline retrieves the correct documents (verified by inspecting the retriever output); but the LLM's final answer frequently contradicts or ignores the retrieved context. Which component of the pipeline is MOST likely causing the issue; and what is the fix?
options: The embedding model is low quality — replace it with a larger embedding model
The prompt template for the generation step is poorly constructed — revise it to explicitly instruct the model to base its answer on the provided context and quote relevant passages
The vector search index has incorrect metadata filters — reconfigure the index
The chunk size is too large — reduce it to 64 tokens
questions.explanation: Since the retriever returns correct documents; the problem lies in the generation step. A poorly constructed prompt may not sufficiently instruct the model to ground its answer in the retrieved context. Revising the prompt to include explicit instructions like 'Answer ONLY based on the context below' and 'Quote relevant passages' directly addresses the faithfulness issue.

questions.question_text: A team deploys a RAG application backed by a Databricks Vector Search index using the Delta Sync mode. After updating source documents in the Delta table; users report that search results still return outdated content for several hours. What is the MOST likely cause and resolution?
options: The embedding model has cached old vectors — restart the embedding model
The Delta Sync index pipeline has not yet triggered a sync — check the sync status and consider switching to a triggered or continuous sync mode for fresher results
The LLM is caching previous answers — clear the LLM cache
The users' browsers are caching old responses — instruct users to clear their cache
questions.explanation: Delta Sync indexes periodically sync changes from the source Delta table. If the sync interval is long; updated documents will not be reflected in search results until the next sync completes. Checking the sync status and configuring a more frequent or continuous sync mode ensures fresher content. The issue is not with LLM caching or browser caching but with the vector index update pipeline.

questions.question_text: A developer is debugging a LangChain agent that sometimes enters an infinite loop — the agent repeatedly calls the same tool with identical arguments without progressing toward an answer. Which TWO fixes are MOST effective?
options: Add a max_iterations parameter to the agent executor to force termination after a set number of steps
Remove all tools so the agent can only use its internal knowledge
Add an output parser that validates tool outputs and injects a 'move on' instruction if the same tool is called more than twice consecutively
Increase the LLM temperature to 2.0 to add randomness
Disable all logging to reduce overhead
questions.explanation: Setting max_iterations prevents infinite loops by capping the number of reasoning-action cycles. Adding an output parser that detects repeated identical tool calls and modifies the prompt to encourage the agent to try a different approach addresses the root cause. Removing tools defeats the purpose of the agent; extreme temperature causes incoherent outputs; and disabling logging removes debugging visibility.

questions.question_text: A developer calls the Databricks Foundation Model API to generate product descriptions and receives intermittent HTTP 429 (rate limit) errors during peak hours. Which strategy BEST ensures reliable operation?
options: Catch 429 errors and immediately retry without any delay
Implement exponential backoff with jitter on retries and consider provisioned throughput for predictable peak-hour traffic
Switch to a completely different LLM provider whenever a 429 error occurs
Reduce the max_tokens parameter to 1 to minimize rate-limit consumption
questions.explanation: HTTP 429 indicates rate limiting. Exponential backoff with jitter spaces out retries to avoid thundering-herd problems. For predictable peak-hour demand; provisioned throughput on Databricks Model Serving guarantees dedicated capacity. Immediate retries worsen congestion; switching providers is operationally complex; and reducing max_tokens to 1 makes responses useless.

questions.question_text: A team has developed a RAG chain logged as an MLflow pyfunc model and now needs to deploy it to production on Databricks. The chain must handle real-time user queries with sub-second latency. Which deployment approach is MOST appropriate?
options: Schedule a Databricks job to run the chain in batch mode every hour
Deploy the MLflow model to a Databricks Model Serving endpoint configured for real-time inference with GPU-enabled instances
Export the model as a static ONNX file and serve it from a local laptop
Deploy the model using only a Databricks notebook with a webhook trigger
questions.explanation: Databricks Model Serving endpoints are purpose-built for real-time inference with auto-scaling; load balancing; and GPU support. Deploying the MLflow pyfunc model to a serving endpoint provides the sub-second latency required. Batch jobs introduce hour-long delays; ONNX export on a laptop is not production-grade; and notebook-based webhook serving lacks reliability and scalability.

questions.question_text: An e-commerce company's LLM-powered product recommendation chatbot experiences 10x traffic spikes during flash sales. During the last sale; the serving endpoint became overloaded and returned timeout errors. Which scaling configuration change MOST effectively addresses this?
options: Set a fixed number of replicas equal to the peak traffic expectation at all times
Configure auto-scaling on the Model Serving endpoint with appropriate min and max replica counts and a scale-to-zero policy for off-peak periods
Deploy a second identical endpoint and manually switch DNS during sales
Reduce the model's context window to speed up inference during spikes
questions.explanation: Auto-scaling with configured min/max replicas allows the serving endpoint to dynamically add capacity during traffic spikes and scale down (even to zero) during quiet periods; optimizing both reliability and cost. Fixed peak provisioning wastes resources during normal traffic. Manual DNS switching is error-prone. Reducing context window degrades answer quality.

questions.question_text: A product team wants to compare two prompt variations for their deployed summarization model to determine which produces higher user satisfaction scores. Both prompts use the same underlying foundation model. Which approach allows a rigorous production comparison?
options: Run both prompts in a notebook on a sample of 10 inputs and eyeball the results
Configure a Model Serving endpoint with traffic splitting to route 50% of live traffic to each prompt variant; then analyze user feedback and quality metrics from inference tables
Deploy only the new prompt and compare results to historical logs from the old prompt
Ask the LLM itself to judge which prompt is better
questions.explanation: Traffic splitting on a Model Serving endpoint enables a controlled A/B test in production with real user queries. Inference tables capture inputs; outputs; and latency for both variants; enabling rigorous statistical comparison of user satisfaction and quality metrics. Notebook evaluation on 10 samples is not statistically valid; historical comparison introduces confounders; and LLM self-evaluation lacks user-preference signal.

questions.question_text: A RAG application is in production and the team suspects the LLM is generating answers that are not faithful to the retrieved context. They need to quantify this problem. Which evaluation approach MOST directly measures faithfulness?
options: Calculate BLEU scores between the generated answer and the retrieved context
Use an LLM-as-judge evaluation that checks whether every claim in the generated answer is supported by the retrieved context and flags unsupported claims
Measure the cosine similarity between the answer embedding and the query embedding
Count the number of tokens in the generated answer
questions.explanation: Faithfulness evaluation requires checking whether the generated answer's claims are grounded in the retrieved context. An LLM-as-judge approach (as used in frameworks like RAGAS or MLflow evaluate) systematically decomposes the answer into individual claims and verifies each against the context. BLEU measures n-gram overlap which does not capture semantic faithfulness; cosine similarity measures relevance not faithfulness; and token count is unrelated.

questions.question_text: A deployed customer-service bot occasionally generates confident-sounding but factually incorrect answers (hallucinations). The team needs an automated detection strategy. Which combination of techniques is MOST effective?
options: Implement a post-generation self-consistency check that samples multiple answers at low temperature and flags responses where answers diverge significantly
Add a retrieval-grounding check that verifies each claim against source documents
Monitor user thumbs-down feedback as the sole detection method
Log all interactions and periodically review a random sample manually
Rely entirely on the system prompt instruction 'do not hallucinate'
questions.explanation: Self-consistency checking (sampling multiple responses and detecting divergence) catches cases where the model is uncertain but sounds confident. Retrieval-grounding verification ensures claims are supported by source documents. User feedback alone is lagging and incomplete. Manual review does not scale. Prompt instructions are helpful but unreliable as a sole safeguard against hallucination.

questions.question_text: A team has deployed an LLM application on Databricks Model Serving and wants to continuously monitor output quality in production. They observe that quality degrades over time as user query patterns shift. Which Databricks-native monitoring strategy is MOST appropriate?
options: Manually read a sample of logs every Friday and report issues in a spreadsheet
Enable inference tables on the serving endpoint to capture all requests and responses; then use Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring to track quality metrics; detect drift; and trigger alerts
Redeploy the model weekly regardless of whether quality has changed
Add a feedback button to the UI and only investigate if complaints exceed a threshold
questions.explanation: Inference tables automatically log all serving endpoint inputs and outputs to a Delta table. Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring can then compute quality metrics (e.g.; toxicity; relevance; faithfulness via LLM judge); detect distribution drift in queries or responses; and trigger alerts when metrics degrade. This provides continuous; automated monitoring. Manual weekly reviews are too infrequent; blind redeployment wastes resources; and feedback-only monitoring misses issues users do not report.

questions.question_text: A data scientist wants to create a feature table to use in their models. They are working in a workspace with Unity Catalog enabled and want this feature table to be stored and governed by it. What is the correct way of creating this feature table?
options: Create a Delta table with data in it, as usual, then use the register_table method from the FeatureStoreClient in Python to register it as a feature table in Unity Catalog.
Create an empty Delta table on Unity Catalog with the AS FEATURE STORE clause via SQL, then write data to it.
Use the create_table method of the FeatureEngineeringClient in Python to create the table, then write data to it.
Create a Delta table with data in it in Unity Catalog then use the ALTER TABLE command in SQL to configure it as a feature table with the SET AS FEATURE STORE clause.
questions.explanation: No Unity Catalog usa-se o create_table do FeatureEngineeringClient (databricks-feature-engineering) para criar a feature table e então gravar os dados. O FeatureStoreClient.register_table pertence ao feature store de workspace (legado) e não existe cláusula SQL 'AS FEATURE STORE'.

questions.question_text: A data scientist needs to impute the missing values in a continuous feature. They want to do this with the least amount of effort but with correct results. Which strategy will do this?
options: Use sklearn SimpleImputer, which automatically selects the best methodology based on the feature distribution
Examine the distribution of the values and select the appropriate imputation upon review
Use .mean(), which is the most appropriate imputation on continuous columns
Use .mode(), which is the most appropriate imputation on continuous columns
questions.explanation: Para resultados corretos, examine a distribuição antes de escolher: a média é sensível a outliers/assimetria, enquanto a mediana é mais robusta. O SimpleImputer não escolhe a estratégia automaticamente, e a moda é para variáveis categóricas. Avaliar a distribuição e selecionar a imputação adequada é o caminho correto.

questions.question_text: A data scientist is developing a model that predicts whether a customer will churn from a subscription service. The dataset is highly imbalanced, with only 10% of the instances representing customers who churn. They want the model to effectively identify the minority class without being biased towards the majority class. Which strategy directly mitigates the model's bias towards the non-churn customers due to class imbalance?
options: Normalize the features to ensure they are on the same scale, improving model performance.
Use cost-sensitive learning by assigning a higher misclassification cost to the minority class during model training.
Increase the size of the training dataset by collecting more data on non-churn customers.
Use a simpler model to reduce overfitting, ensuring it generalizes better to the minority class.
questions.explanation: Aprendizado sensível a custo (class weights / maior custo de classificação errada para a classe minoritária) ataca diretamente o viés em favor da classe majoritária. Normalizar features, coletar mais dados da classe majoritária ou simplificar o modelo não tratam o desbalanceamento.

questions.question_text: A data scientist is tuning a Support Vector Machine (SVM) model using 5-fold cross-validation and GridSearchCV in scikit-learn. The parameter grid includes three hyperparameters: C with values [0.1, 1, 10], kernel with choices ['linear', 'rbf'], and gamma with values [0.01, 0.1, 1]. How many different models will be trained in total?
options: None of the above.
questions.explanation: São 3 (C) × 2 (kernel) × 3 (gamma) = 18 combinações de hiperparâmetros. Com validação cruzada de 5 folds, cada combinação treina 5 modelos: 18 × 5 = 90 modelos no total.

questions.question_text: A podcast platform with thousands of users implemented an anomaly detection algorithm to detect low engagement based on a 10-minute running window of user events (listening, pausing, exiting). A machine learning engineer wants to deploy this model into a production pipeline that must handle up to tens of thousands of events per second, with compute that resizes dynamically as event volume fluctuates. Which pipeline design approach meets these requirements?
options: Create a Delta Live Tables pipeline that applies the algorithm as a Spark UDF.
Create a Structured Streaming Job that applies the algorithm as a Spark UDF.
Create a model serving endpoint, create a Delta Live Tables pipeline that calls a custom UDF which invokes the endpoint.
Create a model serving endpoint, create a Structured Streaming job that calls a custom UDF which invokes the endpoint.
questions.explanation: Uma pipeline Delta Live Tables aplicando o algoritmo como Spark UDF processa o streaming de alto volume e oferece autoescala dinâmica de compute (enhanced autoscaling) conforme o volume varia. Chamar um endpoint de serving por evento adiciona latência/overhead desnecessários para esse caso de janela de 10 minutos.
